Metabolism, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\nFind articles by [Lingxia Cao](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Cao%20L\"\\[Author\\])\n2, [Xumei Li](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Li%20X\"\\[Author\\])\n### Xumei Li\n1Department of Clinical Nutrition, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\nFind articles by [Xumei Li](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Li%20X\"\\[Author\\])\n1, [Dongxu Hong](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Hong%20D\"\\[Author\\])\n### Dongxu Hong\n1Department of Clinical Nutrition, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\nFind articles by [Dongxu Hong](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Hong%20D\"\\[Author\\])\n1, [Suyan Tian](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Tian%20S\"\\[Author\\])\n### Suyan Tian\n3Division of Clinical Research, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\nFind articles by [Suyan Tian](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Tian%20S\"\\[Author\\])\n3,\u2709, [Chenglin Sun](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Sun%20C\"\\[Author\\])\n### Chenglin Sun\n1Department of Clinical Nutrition, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\n2Department of Endocrinology and Metabolism, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\nFind articles by [Chenglin Sun](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=\"Sun%20C\"\\[Author\\])\n1,2,\u2709\n  * Author information\n  * Article notes\n  * Copyright and License information\n\n1Department of Clinical Nutrition, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\n2Department of Endocrinology and Metabolism, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\n3Division of Clinical Research, First Hospital of Jilin University, 1 Xinmin Street, Changchun, 130021 Jilin, China\nAcademic Editor: Hou De Zhou\n\u2709\nCorresponding author.\nReceived 2021 Nov 24; Revised 2022 Feb 18; Accepted 2022 Mar 11; Collection date 2022.\nCopyright \u00a9 2022 Xiaojie Yuan et al.\nThis is an open access article distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution License, which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.\n[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)\nPMCID: PMC8970877 PMID: [35371260](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35371260/)\n##  Abstract\nThe question of whether or not intermittent fasting diets improve the clinical indicators of glycolipid metabolism remains unclear. This study systematically reviewed the relevant clinical trials to evaluate the effects of intermittent fasting diet on glucose and lipid metabolism and insulin sensitivity in patients with metabolic syndrome. To evaluate the effect of intermittent fasting diet intervention on patients with disorders of glucose and lipid metabolism, random-effect or fixed-effect meta-analysis models were used to calculate the average difference before and after intermittent fasting diet intervention and the corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs). After intermittent fasting diet intervention, in terms of glucose metabolism, fasting blood glucose reduced by 0.15 mmol/L (95% CI: \u22120.23; \u22120.06), glycosylated hemoglobin reduced by 0.08 (95% CIs: \u22120.25; \u22120.10), insulin plasma levels reduced by 13.25 uUI (95% CIs: \u221216.69; \u22129.82), and HOMA-IR decreased by 0.31 on an average (95% CIs: \u22120.44; \u22120.19). In addition, BMI decreased by 0.8 kg/m2 (95% CIs: \u22121.32; \u22120.28), body weight reduced by 1.87 kg (95% CIs: \u22122.67; \u22121.07), and the waist circumference decreased by 2.08 cm (95% CIs: \u22123.06; \u22121.10). Analysis of lipid metabolism showed that intermittent fasting diet intervention effectively reduced the total cholesterol level by 0.32 mmol/L (95% CIs: \u22120.60; \u22120.05), low-density lipoprotein level by 0.22 mmol/L (95% CIs: \u22120.37; \u22120.07), and triglyceride level by 0.04 mmol/L (95% CIs: \u22120.15; \u22120.07). Intermittent fasting diets have certain therapeutic effects on blood glucose and lipids in patients with metabolic syndrome and significantly improve insulin resistance. It may be considered as an auxiliary treatment to prevent the occurrence and development of chronic diseases.\n##  1. Introduction\nChronic diseases such as hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, hypertension, and other diseases seriously affect the health condition. The abovementioned factors collectively contributed to metabolic syndrome (MS), which increases the risk of atherosclerotic diseases, cardiovascular disease, insulin resistance, and diabetes, as well as vascular and neurological complications (such as cerebrovascular accidents) [[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B1)\u2013[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B3)]. In the United States, it was estimated that the prevalence of MS was 34.7% by the year 2016 [[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B4)]. The common pathological management of glucose and lipid metabolism is based on weight reduction, especially insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia caused by central obesity. Insensitivity and resistance to insulin are also important pathogenic mechanisms of type 2 diabetes [[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B5)]. Effective dietary interventions are a preventive measure that can promote weight loss, improve glucose and lipid metabolism, improve insulin resistance, and prevent the occurrence and development of diabetes and cardiovascular diseases [[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B6)].\nIntermittent fasting (IF) has been proposed as an alternative to restricted calorie diets as a means of weight control [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B7)]. The IF diet includes various fasting patterns, such as alternate-day fasting (consuming no calories on fasting days), alternate day-modified fasting (consuming less than 25% of caloric requirements on fasting days), time-restricted fasting (restricting food intake at specific times of the day), and periodic fasting (fasting on one to two days per week). A retrospective study [[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B8)] found that IF diets produce gradual weight loss, lower blood pressure, have anticarcinogenic effects, and may even extend the lifespan. IF diets are not simply about enduring starvation, they represent a healthier lifestyle choice. A recent meta-analysis [[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B7)] reviewed the clinical studies of IF diets and found that they reduce adult body mass index (BMI) and insulin resistance. To the best of our knowledge, however, there have been no meta-analyses targeting clinical studies of the effects of IF diets on impaired glucose and lipid metabolism. IF diets have produced conflicting results in clinical studies of patients with impaired glucose and lipid metabolism. In support of IF, Sutton et al. [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B9)] found that 5 weeks on an IF diet significantly improved the insulin sensitivity and blood pressure of male participants with impaired glucose and lipid metabolism. Furmli et al. [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B10)] found that IF reversed and/or reduced insulin resistance while significantly reducing body weight, waist circumference, and HbA1c [[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B10)]. It also contributed to the maintenance of stable blood glucose levels after discontinuation of insulin use. In contrast, Alghamdi et al. [[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B11)] studied the effects of IF during Ramadan on glucose metabolism in patients with MS and found that HbA1c increased by 0.11% after a month. Harvie et al. [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B12), [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B13)] found that 3 months on an IF diet significantly reduced the total cholesterol, triglyceride (TG), and low-density lipoprotein (LDL) levels of patients with impaired glucose and lipid metabolism. However, Sutton et al. [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B9)] reported contradictory results, with increases in total cholesterol and TG in this demographic after 2 months of IF.\nIn the present study, we aimed to summarize clinical trials on the effects of IF diet intervention on glucose and lipid metabolism of patients with MS-impaired glucose and lipid metabolism in recent years worldwide, thereby exploring whether or not IF diets improve the clinical indicators of glycolipid metabolism. The current meta-analysis evaluated the actual efficacy of the IF diet using objective data and reported the effects of the IF diet on blood glucose, HbA1c and whether or not this intervention improves lipid metabolism and insulin sensitivity.\n##  2. Methods and Materials\n### 2.1. Literature Search\nOnly previous publications written in English were considered. A literature search was performed in the PubMed and MEDLINE databases by using the following keywords: intermittent fasting diet, obesity/overweight, randomized controlled trial, MS, and human. The end date of this literature search was June 5th, 2020. This meta-analysis was planned and performed following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analysis Guideline ([Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#fig1)). MS was defined as the presence of any of the following metabolic dysfunctions: obesity, hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, or hypertension. Overweight was defined as BMI >25 kg/m2, and obesity was defined as BMI >30 kg/m2 according to the World Health Organization standard.\n#### Figure 1.\n![Figure 1](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/b4d0/8970877/2af95156f5c6/IJE2022-6999907.001.jpg)\n[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/figure/fig1/)\nPRISMA diagram for the systematic review.\n### 2.2. Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria\nInclusion criteria included the following: (1) the therapeutic dietary under consideration is IF; (2) the study was carried out on humans; and (3) the summary statistics of the mean difference between before and during the IF intervention is available (if the means before and during the IF diet is available, then the difference was taken to obtain the desired mean difference), along with their corresponding standard error or 95% confidence intervals (CIs) or _p_ values. Based on either of the last two statistics, the corresponding statistics (i.e., the corresponding standard deviation of the mean difference) can be calculated. Given that many included studies are longitudinal, several time points may be retrieved in a single study.\nExclusion criteria included the following: (1) case reports, (2) meta-analyses or reviews, (3) studies of normal weight or lean subjects, (4) inadequate information to retrieve (or calculate) the necessary summary statistics, (5) data covering periods longer than 3 months, and (6) reviews, letters, conference abstracts, editorials, and commentaries.\nThree of the authors (X.Y, J.W, and S.Y) independently reviewed the titles, abstracts, and full-text studies to determine eligibility for inclusion. Any disagreements were addressed by discussion or consultation with Dr. Sun and Dr. Tian.\n### 2.3. Statistical Analysis\nThe effects of IF on MS were estimated by the mean difference before and after IF implementation and their corresponding 95% CIs in random-effect or fixed-effect meta-analysis models, which were determined through the heterogeneity of studies. Heterogeneity among studies was evaluated by Cochrane's _Q_ statistic and the _I_ 2 statistical methods. If the corresponding _p_ value was <0.05 and _I_ 2 > 0.5, a random-effect meta-analysis model was used. Otherwise, a fixed-effect meta-analysis model was chosen. To evaluate the possibility of potential bias, funnel plots in which effect sizes versus standard errors were diagramed and were made for each outcome and visually inspected. We further assessed for publication bias by using Egger's regression tests. All statistical analyses were carried out in the R software, version 3.5 (<https://www.r-project.org>).\n##  3. Results\nIn total, we identified 157 potentially relevant citations from the PubMed and MEDLINE databases. After screening the titles and/or abstracts using our inclusion and exclusion criteria, 139 articles were excluded. The full text of 18 articles was evaluated. Of these, seven were excluded because of missing information (the summary statistics could not be retrieved), and one was excluded because of the study duration, as it was an evaluation of the long-term effects of IF. The remaining 10 articles fulfilled our criteria and were included in our meta-analysis. These comprised 10 randomized controlled trials with 12 types of intervention [[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B9), [11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B11)\u2013[19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#B19)]. Our search and screening process is summarized in [Figure 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#fig1). Details such as the IF diet type and follow-up duration are summarized in [Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8970877/#tab1). The effects of the IF diet on glucose metabolism, insulin resistance, weight loss, lipid metabolism, and blood pressure control of patients with impaired glucose and lipid metabolism were evaluated.\n### Table 1.\nSummary of participants' characteristics of studies included.\n| Study | Country | Study subdivision |  _N_ | Follow up | Dietary intervention | Age | BMI (kg/m2) | Population  \n---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---  \n1 | Sutton et al. The variables measured to determine glucose metabolism included fasting glucose, insulin, HbA1c, and HOMA-IR levels, whereas TG, high-density lipoprotein (HDL), and LDL levels were used to assess lipid metabolism. Weight loss was measured using body weight, BMI, and waist circumference. In the meta-analysis, fixed-effect models were fit for BMI, waist circumference, SBP, HDL-C, HOMA-IR, and insulin, whereas random-effects models were adopted for other parameters according to their Cochrane's _Q_ and _I_ 2 statistics.\nThe fasting blood glucose level decreased by 0.15 mmol/L (95% CIs: \u22120.19; \u22120.11) after the IF diet. Concerning glycosylated hemoglobin, no significant change in HbAlc was found after the IF intervention even though the mean difference reduced by 0.08%. Insulin level decreased by 13.25 mU/L (95% CIs: -16.69; \u22129.82) on average after the IF intervention, whereas HOMA-IR decreased by 0.31 (95% CIs: \u22120.44; \u22120.19). The forest plots for these two carb"
outputs\codex_findings\v27_deep_content_audit\_codex_stdout.txt:3109:outputs\polaris_graph\PG_TEST_086.json:2144:      "content": "Title: Paper2+Dr.+Uttam+Banik+Physiology+Scopus.pdf\n\nURL Source: https://jchr.org/index.php/JCHR/article/download/9412/5234/17841\n\nNumber of Pages: 8\n\nMarkdown Content:\n1616 \n\n# Journal of Chemical Health Risks \n\nwww.jchr.org \n\nJCHR (20 25 ) 15 (4), 1616 -1623 | ISSN:2251 -6727 \n\n# Effect of Intermittent Fasting on Insulin Sensitivity: A Meta -Analysis \n\n1. Dr. Uttam Banik , Assistant Professor, Department of Physiology, Agartala Government Medical College & GB Pant \n\nHospital, Agartala, Tripura, India \n\nCorresponding Author: Dr. Uttam Banik \n\nReceived: 11 June 2025 . Revised: 25 June 2025 Accepted: 2 8 July 2025 \n\nKEYWORDS \n\nIntermittent \n\nfasting\u2019, \n\n\u2018Insulin \n\nsensitivity\u2019, \n\n\u2018HOMA -IR\u2019, \n\n\u2018Time -\n\nrestricted \n\nfeeding\u2019, \n\n\u2018Type 2\n\ndiabetes\u2019. \n\nABSTRACT: \n\nBackground: The development of \"type 2 diabetes mellitus\" and other \"metabolic processes \n\nillnesses\" is significantly influenced by \"insulin intolerance.\" As a dietary approach to enhance \n\n\"digestion health,\" \"sporadic fasting,\" which includes \"time -restrict ed consumption,\" \"alternate -day \n\nfasting,\" and \"5:2 diet patterns,\" has grown in preference. Information about its effect on glucose \n\ntolerance is still conflicting, though. \n\nMethods: Up to May 2025, a thorough literature search was conducted in \"PubMed,\" \"Embase,\" \n\n\"CRUCIAL,\" as well as \"Scopus.\" The requirements for participation were satisfied by 16 RCTs \n\nwith 1,671 people. Two reviewers each carried out the \"data retrieval\" a s well as \"the likelihood of \n\nbias\" assessments. Insulin empathy, as determined by HOMA -IR, fasting insulin, or hypoglycemia \n\nclamp methods, was the main result. Using \"RevMan 5.4,\" a \"random -effects model\" was used to \n\ndo the \"meta -analysis.\" \n\nResults:With a pooled mean disparity in HOMA -IR of -0.72 (95% CI: -1.03 to -0.41; p < 0.001), \n\nIF significantly enhanced the susceptibility to insulin. Subgroup studies showed that 24 -hourly rate \n\neating and early TRF led to larger benefits. These advantage s were mostly unrelated to weight loss. \n\nThe results were robust, as evidenced by sensitivity evaluations, and some variability was noted (I2 \n\n= 52%). Conclusion: It seems that intermittent fasting, especially full -day fasting alongside early \n\ntime -restricted eating, can improve insulin sensitivity in a variety of groups. To identify the best \n\npractices along with supporting processes, more extensive longitudinal research are required. \n\nINTRODUCTION \n\nIn recent years, the prevalence of insulin resistance and \n\ntype 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) has dramatically grown \n\nworldwide, raising serious public health concerns. (IR), \n\na key factor in the pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes, \n\nmanifests as a diminished phys iologic response to the \n\nhormone insulin and is strongly linked to obesity, \n\ndyslipidaemia, and cardiovascular disorders [1]. Making \n\ndietary and lifestyle changes is a key strategy for \n\n\"bettering the response to insulin as well as lessening the \n\nchance of dig estive disorders.\" \n\nIntermittent fasting (IF) has garnered a lot of attention as \n\na dietary approach that may offer physiologic benefits \n\nexceeding those achieved by traditional calorie reduction. \n\nEating routines that occasional amongst eating at \n\ndifferent times are referred to as \"IF\"; they include time -\n\nrestricted feeding (TRF), alternate -day fasting (ADF), \n\nand the 5:2 diet. [2]. \"IF focusses on the frequency of \n\nfood consumption etc might impact insulin release, \n\nglucose absorption, as well as biological cycles in \n\ncomparison wit h continual calorie restriction.\" \n\nNumerous mechanistic studies have shown that IF can \n\nenhance the response to insulin by modifying adipokine \n\nlevels, reducing cellular inflammation, and improving \n\nmitochondrial function [3]. \n\n\"The effects of IF on human insulin sensitivity are still \n\nnot entirely clear, despite the increasing amount of \n\nresearch on the subject. While some randomised \n\ncontrolled studies (RCTs) have found no discernible \n\nchange when compared to conventional diets [6] , others \n\nhave observed notable increases in insulin sensitivity \n\nafter IF therapies [4,5]. These contradictory findings are \n\ncaused by differences in insulin sensitivity assessment \n\ntechniques, demographic characteristics, intervention \n\nlength, and fasting reg imens. Therefore, it is crucial to \n\ncarry out a systematic review and meta -analysis in order \n\nto compile the current body of evidence and elucidate \n\nhow intermittent fasting affects insulin sensitivity. 1617 \n\n# Journal of Chemical Health Risks \n\nwww.jchr.org \n\nJCHR (20 25 ) 15 (4), 1616 -1623 | ISSN:2251 -6727 \n\nThe purpose of this \"systematic evaluation\" along with \n\n\"meta -analysis\" is to assess how different \"infrequent \n\nfasting\" protocols affect individuals' insulin sensitivity. \n\nThis study aims to determine if IF provides notable \n\nmetabolic benefits and to pinpoint variables that could \n\naffect its efficacy by combining results from RCTs. The \n\nreview's findings may have significant ramifications for \n\ndietary guidelines and initiatives pertaining to public \n\nhealth aimed at addressing insulin resistance and its \n\nassociated consequences. \n\nTECHNIQUES \n\n\"The Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews \n\nand Meta -Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines were followed \n\nin the conduct of this systematic review and meta -\n\nanalysis.\" [1]. To guarantee openness and repeatability, a \n\nthorough review procedure was created bef ore to the \n\nstudy's start, detailing the goals, eligibility requirements, \n\nsearch technique, and analytic plan. \n\nStudies that were \"randomised controlled trials (RCTs)\" \n\nincluding persons 18 years of age and older, irrespective \n\nof gender or initial metabolic state, were deemed suitable. \n\nAny kind of \"intermittent fasting (IF), such as time -\n\nrestricted feeding (TRF), alt ernate -day fasting (ADF), or \n\nthe 5:2 regimen,\" was the main treatment that was \n\nassessed. Studies required to provide results pertaining to \n\ninsulin resistance and compare IF with either a\n\nconventional diet, continuous restricting calories, or \n\nbackground lev els in order to be included. \"Insulin \n\nsensitivity measurements included fasting insulin \n\nconcentrations, the hyperinsulinemic -euglycemic clamp \n\nmethod, or the Homeostatic Model Assessment for \n\nInsulin Resistance (HOMA -IR).\" The inclusion criteria \n\nwere limited to \"full -text articles published in peer -\n\nreviewed journals and written in the English language.\" \n\nSurveys, evaluation articles, conference proceedings, \n\nreports of incidents, along with editorials were not \n\nincluded in the study. \n\nUsing electronic databases such PubMed (MEDLINE), \n\nEmbase, the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled \n\nTrials (CENTRAL), and Scopus, a comprehensive the \n\nterm \"literature search was conducted out, covering all \n\nrecords up to May 2025.\" \"Medical Subject Headin gs \n\n(MeSH) and pertinent free -text keywords related to \n\ninsulin sensitivity and fasting between meals were \n\ncombined in the search strategy.\" Filters were performed \n\nto locate randomised controlled studies using the \n\nfollowing terms: \"irregular fasting,\" \"time -restricted \n\nfeeding,\" \"alternate -day fasting,\" \"insulin \n\nresponsiveness,\" \"HOMA -IR,\" and \"glucose clamp.\" \n\nReference lists of relevant papers and papers were \n\nexamined by hand to find any more relevant research to \n\nadd to the database search. \n\n\"Two reviewers carried out a full -text review after \n\nscreening titles and abstracts.\" Any disagreements were \n\nsettled by talking things out or \"consulting an additional \n\nreview.\" \"The choice procedure was laid out utilising an \n\norderly diagram and complied wit h the PRISMA 2020 \n\nguidelines.\" [7]. \n\nData was separately retrieved by two reviewers using a \n\npre -made, standardised form. Participant demographics \n\n(sample size, age, body mass index, and health condition), \n\nstudy characteristics (author names, publication year, \n\nand country), intervention detail s (type of duration of \n\nintermittent fasting), comparator group details, outcome \n\nindicators (HOMA -IR, fasting insulin, and glucose \n\nclamp results), and primary findings were among the data \n\ngathered. Any discrepancies between reviewers were \n\nsettled by discuss ion and consensus. Using the \n\n\"Cochrane Risk of Bias 2.0 (RoB 2) tool,\" which \n\nevaluates several domains, including \"randomisation \n\nprocess,\" \"adherence to assigned interventions,\" \n\n\"completeness of outcome data,\" \"accuracy of outcome \n\nmeasurement,\" and \"select ive reporting,\" the \n\nmethodological quality potential bias in the chosen \n\nstudies was assessed [2]. \n\nThe \"Review Manager (RevMan ) software, version 5.4\" \n\nwas used to do the meta -analysis. The \"mean difference \n\n(MD) or standardised mean difference (SMD)\" and \"95% \n\nconfidence intervals (CIs)\" were computed for \n\ncontinuous variables. \"A random -effects model was \n\nemployed\" because of the an ticipated variation in \n\nparticipant demographics and fasting regimens between \n\nstudies. The \"I\u00b2 statistic\" was used to assess statistical \n\n\"heterogeneity,\" with limits of 25 percent, 50 percent, \n\nand 75% denoting small, moderate, and significant \n\nheterogeneity, correspondingly. According on the \n\nrespondents' preexisting medical problems, the kind of \n\ndaily fasting schedule, and the length of the intervention, \n\npredetermined sub group analyses were conducted. \n\nTo test the \u2018robustness of findings\u2019, the \u2018sensitivity \n\nanalyses\u2019 were performed by removing studies identified \n\nas having a \u2018high risk of bias\u2019. Assessment of \u2018potential 1618 \n\n# Journal of Chemical Health Risks \n\nwww.jchr.org \n\nJCHR (20 25 ) 15 (4), 1616 -1623 | ISSN:2251 -6727 \n\npublication bias\u2019 was done using \u2018funnel plot asymmetry \n\nand Egger\u2019s regression test\u2019 when at least ten studies \n\nwere included. This systematic review was registered \n\nwith the \u2018International Prospective Register of \n\nSystematic Reviews (PROSPERO)\u2019 under registr ation \n\nID [CRD420251065593]. \n\nRESULTS \n\nUsing database searches, 1,432 records were found in \n\ntotal. 1,035 titles and abstracts were examined after 397 \n\nduplicates were eliminated. 83 full -text papers were \n\nevaluated for eligibility after this preliminary screening; \n\n75 reports were obtained, and 59 reports were \n\ndisqualified. The systematic review and meta -analysis \n\nincluded 16 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with \n\n1,671 individuals that finally satisfied the inclusion \n\ncriteria. The PRISMA 2020 flow diagram (Figure 1) \n\nprovides specifics on the rese arch selection procedure. \n\nUsing database searches, 1,432 records were found in \n\ntotal. 1,035 titles and abstracts were examined after 397 \n\nduplicates were eliminated. 83 full -text papers were \n\nevaluated for eligibility after this preliminary screening; \n\n75 reports were obtained, and 59 reports were \n\ndisqualified. The systematic review and meta -analysis \n\nincluded 16 randomised controlled trials (RCTs) with \n\n1,671 individuals that finally satisfied the inclusion \n\ncriteria. The PRISMA 2020 flow diagram (Figure 1) \n\nprovides specifics on the rese arch selection procedure .\n\nFigure 1. PRISMA 2020 flow diagram for systematic \n\nreviews which included searches of databases \n\nWhen compared to control groups, intermittent fasting \n\nsignificantly improved insulin sensitivity, according to \n\npooled analysis. Intermittent fasting treatments resulted \n\nin a weighted mean difference (WMD) of \u22120.72 (\u201895% \n\nConfidence Interval [CI]: \u22121.03 to \u2212 0.41; p < 0.001\u2019) \n\nacross 14 trials that reported HOMA -IR as an endpoint. \n\nThis suggests that intermittent fasting significantly \n\nreduces insulin resistance. Subgroup studies showed that \n\nHOMA -IR and fasting insulin levels were consistently \n\nsignificantly reduc ed by time -restricted feeding \n\nschedules with meal windows of 6 to 8 hours. Although \n\nthe results varied based on participant commitment, \n\nalternate -day fasting regimens also shown modest \n\nimprovements in metabolic markers. \n\nSignificant decreases in HOMA -IR were observed by \n\nHorne et al. (2024) and \"Bartholomew et al. (2021)\" that \n\nwere unrelated to weight loss. These results imply that \n\nintermittent fasting's metabolic advantages could go \n\nbeyond merely limiting calorie intake. N otably, a number \n\nof studies carried out in India showed steady \n\nimprovements in glycaemic indicators and insulin \n\nresistance, including notable drops in \"HOMA -induced,\" \n\n\"fasting blood glucose,\" and \"HbA1c levels.\" \n\nFurthermore, compared to evening or unrestri cted meal \n\nregimens, early time -restricted feeding \u2014that is, eating \n\nwindows beginning in the morning \u2014seemed to be more \n\nsuccessful in enhancing insulin sensitivity. The \n\nsecondary study by Horne et al. (2024) revealed a \n\nsurprising finding: after intermittent f asting, HOMA -IR \n\ndecreased more in those with lower baseline human \n\ngrowth hormone (HGH) levels. This implies that basal \n\nhormone \n\nThe secondary study by Horne et al. (2024) revealed a \n\nsurprising finding: after intermittent fasting, HOMA -IR \n\ndecreased more in those with lower baseline human \n\ngrowth hormone (HGH) levels. This highlights a weight -\n\nindependent strategy for the reported bene fits and raises \n\nthe possibility that baseline hormonal state may influence \n\nthe way the body reacts to fasting regimens. \n\nAn I2 score of 52% indicated moderate heterogeneity \n\nacross the included studies. Sensitivity analyses that \n\neliminated studies with a high risk of bias did not \n\nsignificantly change the findings, suggesting that the \n\nconclusions were sound overall. The length of the \n\nintervention and the particular fasting window duration \n\nwere shown to be significant variables to the variation in 1619 \n\n# Journal of Chemical Health Risks \n\nwww.jchr.org \n\nJCHR (20 25 ) 15 (4), 1616 -1623 | ISSN:2251 -6727 \n\neffect sizes among trials, according to meta -analysis \n\nanalysis. The Cochrane Risk of Bias 2.0 tool was used to \n\nevaluate the risk of bias. While several studies raised \n\nsignificant concerns, mostly about randomisation \n\nprocesses and inadequate outcome data, t he majority of \n\nincluded research were judged as having a low risk of \n\nbias. \n\nCrucially, no study was deemed to be very susceptible to \n\nbias. Egger's test showed no indication of publication \n\nbias (p = 0.13), while funnel plot analysis showed no \n\ndiscernible asymmetry. In conclusion, the data indicates \n\nthat insulin sensitivity is consi derably increased in a \n\nvariety of groups by intermittent fasting, especially time -\n\nrestricted meals and 24 -hour fasting regimes. These \n\nadvantages could be impacted by baseline hormonal \n\nprofiles and seem to be unrelated to weight loss. \n\nSr. \n\nNo. \n\nStudy \n\n(Author, \n\nYear) \n\nDesign  Population  Intervention  Compara \n\ntor \n\nPrimary \n\nOutcomes  Findings \n\n1 Soeters et al., \n\n2009 [8] \n\nRCT \n\n(Crossove \n\nr) \n\n8 healthy lean \n\nmen \n\nADF (20h fast \n\nalt. days for 2 \n\nweeks) \n\nStandard \n\nDiet \n\nInsulin \n\nSensitivity \n\n(Clamp) \n\nNo significant effect on \n\ninsulin sensitivity. \n\n2 Varady et al., \n\n2013 [9]  RCT \n\n32 \n\nnormal/overw \n\neight adults \n\nADF  Control  Weight, \n\nCHD Risk \n\nSignificant weight loss and \n\nimproved adipokines, no \n\ndirect insulin sensitivity data. \n\n3 Rastogi et al., \n\n2024 [10]  RCT  273 T2DM \n\npatients \n\nTRM (Dinner \n\nat 7 PM) \n\nLate \n\neating \n\nHbA1c, \n\nFPG, PPG, \n\nBMI \n\nSignificant improvements in \n\nglycemic markers and \n\nweight. 1620 \n\n# Journal of Chemical Health Risks \n\nwww.jchr.org \n\nJCHR (20 25 ) 15 (4), 1616 -1623 | ISSN:2251 -6727 \n\n4 Gabel et al., \n\n2018 [11]  RCT  23 Obese \n\nadults \n\n8-h TRF (10 \n\nAM \u20136 PM) \n\nHistorical \n\nControl \n\nWeight, BP, \n\nHOMA -IR \n\nWeight, BP improved; improvement \n\nindependent of weight loss. \n\n15  Obermayer et \n\nal., 2023 [16] \n\nRCT \n\n(INTERF \n\nAST -2\n\nStudy) \n\n46 Insulin -\n\ntreated T2DM \n\npatients \n\n3\n\nnonconsecutiv \n\ne days/week \n\nIF for 12 \n\nweeks \n\nStandard \n\nCare \n\nHbA1c, \n\nInsulin \n\nDose, Body \n\nWeight \n\nIF group showed significant \n\nHbA1c reduction, insulin \n\ndose reduction, and weight \n\nloss without severe \n\nhypoglycemia. 1621 \n\n# Journal of Chemical Health Risks \n\nwww.jchr.org \n\nJCHR (20 25 ) 15 (4), 1616 -1623 | ISSN:2251 -6727 \n\n16 \n\nBartholomew \n\net al., \n\n2021 [17] \n\nRCT \n\n(WONDE \n\nRFUL \n\nTrial) \n\n103 Adults \n\n(Metabolic \n\nSyndrome \n\nfeatures) \n\n24 -h water -\n\nonly fast \n\n(2x/week for \n\n4 weeks, then \n\n1x/week) \n\nAd \n\nLibitum \n\nControl \n\nLDL -C\n\n(Primary), \n\nHOMA -IR, \n\nMetSyn \n\nScore \n\n(Secondary) \n\nHOMA -IR and MetSyn \n\nScore improved significantly; \n\nLDL -C and cognition \n\nunchanged. \n\nDISCUSSION \n\nThis meta -analysis demonstrates that intermittent fasting \n\n(IF) significantly improves insulin sensitivity, as \n\nreflected by the reduction in \u2018Homeostatic Model \n\nAssessment of Insulin Resistance (HOMA -IR)\u2019, \u2018fasting \n\ninsulin\u2019, and \u2018fasting glucose levels\u2019. Our findings are \n\nconsistent with prior individual randomized controlled \n\ntrials and systematic reviews that have explored the \n\nmetabolic benefits of various IF regimens. \n\nThe overall pooled analysis showed a substantial \n\ndecrease in HOMA -IR, supporting the notion that IF is a \n\nrobust intervention for improving insulin sensitivity. \n\nSimilar trends were reported by Horne et al., who found \n\nthat \u201clow -frequency water -only intermitt ent fasting over \n\n26 weeks significantly reduced HOMA -IR, especially \n\namong individuals with lower baseline human growth \n\nhormone (HGH) levels, suggesting a possible interaction \n\nbetween HGH and metabolic outcomes\u201d [15] . \u201cEarly \n\ntime -restricted feeding (eTRF) also emerges as a potent \n\nstrategy. Sutton et al. conducted a controlled feeding trial \n\nwherein eTRF (6 -hour feeding window with an early \n\ndinner) improved insulin sensitivity, blood pressure, and \n\noxidative stress marke rs in men with prediabetes without \n\nnecessitating weight loss\u201d [14] . These findings align with \n\nour observation that IF benefits extend beyond mere \n\nweight reduction. \n\n\u201cSeveral studies included in this analysis highlight the \n\nimportance of the feeding window. Cienfuegos et al. \n\ncompared 4-hour and 6-hour time -restricted feeding \n\nregimens and reported comparable reductions in weight \n\nand insulin resistance markers\u201d [12] . Similarly, Gabel et \n\nal. demonstrated that \u201can 8-hour feeding window \n\nsignificantly reduced systolic blood pressure and induced \n\nmodest weight loss without notable changes in lipid or \n\ninsulin markers\u201d [11] . Our subgroup analysis further \n\nsupports that time -restricted feeding regimens with \n\nshorter eating windows tend to yield more pronounced \n\nimprovements in insulin sensitivity. Regarding different \n\nIF strategies, the 5:2 diet, involving two days of fasting \n\nper week, has demonstrated efficacy in improving \n\nmetabolic health. Ekberg et al. reported that \u201cthe 5:2 diet \n\nsignificantly reduced fasting insulin, C -peptide, HOMA -\n\nIR, and improved body composition in individuals with \n\nand without type 2 diabetes\u201d [18] . These improvements \n\nwere sustained at 12 months, highlighting the potential \n\nfor long -term metabolic benefits. \n\nThe meta -analysis also underscores \u201cthe advantage of \n\nintermittent fasting over continuous caloric restriction \n\n(CR). Hutchison et al. compared intermittent versus \n\ncontinuous energy intake and found that intermittent \n\nfasting led to greater reductions in weig ht, fat mass, and \n\nlow -density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL -C) despite \n\nequivalent caloric deficits\u201d [13] . Parvaresh et al. \n\nsimilarly found that \u201cmodified alternate -day fasting \n\n(MADF) led to greater reductions in body weight, waist \n\ncircumference, systolic blood pressure, and fasting \n\nglucose than CR in patients with metabolic syndrome\u201d \n\n[19] . The mechanistic basis behind the benefits of IF is \n\nmultifaceted. Vasim et al. proposed that IF enhances fatty \n\nacid metabolism, induces ketogenesis, improves lipid \n\nprofiles, and reduces blood pressure and oxidative stress, \n\ncontributing to overall metaboli c improvements [20] .\n\nAdditionally, IF may favorably influence circadian \n\nrhythms, which are critical regulators of glucose \n\nmetabolism and insulin sensitivity. \n\nDespite these promising results, heterogeneity among \n\nstudies warrants caution. Differences in IF regimens \n\n(\u2018alternate -day fasting\u2019, \u20185:2 diet\u2019, \u2018time -restricted \n\nfeeding\u2019), duration of intervention, baseline metabolic \n\nstatus, and study populations could con tribute to the \n\nvariability in outcomes. While most studies reported \n\nimprovements in insulin sensitivity independent of \n\nweight loss, weight loss likely remains an important co -\n\nfactor enhancing the metabolic benefits of IF. 1622 \n\n# Journal of Chemical Health Risks \n\nwww.jchr.org \n\nJCHR (20 25 ) 15 (4), 1616 -1623 | ISSN:2251 -6727 \n\nStrengths and Limitations \n\nA key strength of this meta -analysis is the inclusion of a \n\nbroad range of IF regimens and a focus on insulin \n\nsensitivity outcomes assessed through standardized \n\nmeasures such as HOMA -IR. Additionally, the findings \n\nare consistent across various subgroups, su ggesting the \n\nrobustness of the results. However, there are limitations \n\nto consider. Variations in study design, intervention \n\nadherence, and dietary intake outside the feeding \n\nwindows could affect outcomes. The relatively short \n\nfollow -up periods in some stu dies limit conclusions \n\nregarding long -term sustainability and safety. \n\nFurthermore, although our analysis focused on adults, the \n\napplicability to different age groups and those with \n\nadvanced metabolic disorders requires further \n\nexploration. \n\nCONCLUSION \n\nIn summary, intermittent fasting shows considerable \n\npotential as an effective strategy for enhancing insulin \n\nsensitivity and overall metabolic health. Approaches \n\nsuch as time -restricted feeding and alternate -day fasting \n\nhave been associated with favorable outcomes, often \n\nindependent of notable weight reduction. These results \n\nsupport the integration of intermittent fasting into \n\nlifestyle -based approaches for the prevention and \n\nmanagement of insulin resistance and type 2 diabetes. \n\nHowever, further well -design ed, long -duration \n\nrandomized controlled trials are necessary to identify the \n\nmost effective fasting protocols, improve adherence, and \n\nclarify the physiological mechanisms driving these \n\nmetabolic improvements. \n\nREFERENCES \n\n1. DeFronzo RA. Insulin resistance, lipotoxicity, \n\ntype 2 diabetes and atherosclerosis: the missing \n\nlinks. The Claude Bernard Lecture 2009. \n\nDiabetologia 2010;53:1270 \u201387. \n\n2. Patterson RE, Laughlin GA, LaCroix AZ, \n\nHartman SJ, Natarajan L, Senger CM, et al. \n\nIntermittent Fasting and Human Metabolic Health. \n\nJ. Acad. Nutr. Diet. 2015;115:1203 \u201312. \n\n3. outputs\codex_findings\m41_code_audit_pass2\_codex_stdout.txt:1308:   `src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:150` still declares the outline JSON array as `4-5 objects`; `:162` still says Mechanism is one of the 5 outline sections; and, more importantly, the invalid-outline retry at `:462-480` appends a hard requirement to `Return EXACTLY 5 sections`. outputs\m6_verify_tech\tech\tech_rag_architectures_2024\report.md:17:A novel paradigm for image generation, AR-RAG, enhances the process by autoregressively incorporating k-nearest neighbor retrievals at the patch level at each generation step, unlike prior static retrieval methods.[10] In code generation, the EVOR pipeline addresses limitations of static knowledge bases by employing the synchronous evolution of both queries and diverse knowledge bases.[11] For automating literature reviews, one approach compares several NLP strategies, finding that a retrieval-augmented generation system using GPT-3.5-turbo achieved the highest ROUGE-1 score of 0.364.[12] A security-focused analysis presents the Interrogation Attack, a stealthy membership inference technique for RAG that demonstrates successful inference with just 30 queries and shows a 2x improvement in TPR@1%FPR over prior attacks.[13] These evolving and autoregressive methods contrast with simpler RAG applications, as AR-RAG uses context-aware retrievals to avoid issues like over-copying, while EVOR aims to improve adaptation to domains with insufficient knowledge.[10][11] The evaluation metrics also vary, from execution accuracy in code generation  to ROUGE scores for text summarization  and security metrics like true positive rate for inference attacks.[11][12][13]

outputs\m5_fixed_tech\tech\tech_rag_architectures_2024\report.md:13:In the medical domain, there is no consensus on the best practices for building a RAG system regarding its components, organization, and implementation for industrial applications.[7] For creative applications like image generation, AR-RAG introduces a novel paradigm that enhances generation by autoregressively incorporating k-nearest neighbor retrievals at the patch level, performing context-aware retrievals at each step to avoid limitations like over-copying.[8] In code generation, the EVOR pipeline addresses limitations of static knowledge bases by employing the synchronous evolution of both queries and diverse knowledge bases, achieving two to four times the execution accuracy compared to other methods on certain benchmarks.[9] For music-related applications, the MusT-RAG framework adapts general-purpose LLMs for text-only music question answering by proposing a music-specialized vector database and utilizing context during both inference and fine-tuning.[10] In academic research, an automated literature review system using RAG with GPT-3.5-turbo achieved a ROUGE-1 score of 0.364, outperforming other NLP strategies like a transformer model and a frequency-based method.[11]

outputs\polaris_graph\PG_TEST_058_report.md:93:The distributional implications of compliance costs across customer classes raise equity considerations that intersect with technology selection. Regressive rate impacts on low-income households may influence utility decisions toward technologies with lower lifecycle costs despite higher capital requirements, particularly where state or federal subsidies bridge initial investment gaps. Conversely, communities with substantial commercial or industrial rate bases may tolerate higher per-unit costs in exchange for treatment reliability and regulatory certainty. These socioeconomic determinants of technology preference receive insufficient attention in purely engineering-economic analyses but substantially shape implementable solutions.
